checklist: Utilize Stack data structures for iterative DFS

states.question: What is the recursion call stack height during a DFS traversal of a straight-line graph of N nodes?
states.explanation: For a linear tree graph, DFS goes all the way down, adding N stack frames onto the recursion call stack, taking O(N) space.

states.analogy_description: You walk forward in a maze. If you hit a dead end, you backtrack to the last intersection, unwind the string, and try the next direction. This is DFS.

whiteboard_data.title: DFS Recursion Stack
whiteboard_data.description: Call stack frames representing active depth path search.

states.exercise_description: Write a recursive function `dfs_traverse(graph, start)` that returns a list of vertices in DFS order (pre-order). Keep a visited set.
Write a function `has_path(graph, start, end)` that returns `True` if a path exists between `start` and `end` using DFS, and `False` otherwise.

states.prompt: Explain Depth-First Search in your own words to a peer. Use an analogy from daily life.

flashcards.front: What is topological sort based on?
flashcards.back: Post-order DFS traversal of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG).

flashcards.front: How do you detect a cycle in a directed graph using DFS?
flashcards.back: By tracking nodes currently in the recursion stack (back edge checking).
